alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same generalization but increases the stated confidence, so the claim content is unchanged while confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results can be used to guide both experimental and computational studies on the role of the GPe for basal ganglia dynamics in health and disease. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work provides insight into the theoretical implications of a dichotomous globus pallidus pars externa organization. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution more narrowly as providing theoretical insight into a dichotomous organization rather than a broad guide for experimental and computational studies, so the contribution wording is revised.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Increased inhibition of prototypical cells by arkypallidal cells leads to the emergence of a bistable regime in the GPe. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific mechanistic claim (arkypallidal→prototypical inhibition causing bistability) is not explicitly stated in the preprint text and thus appears as an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Category-selectivity for faces and written words is largely dissociated in the human ventral occipito-temporal cortex. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These observations support the view that category-selective circuitry for faces and written words is largely dissociated in the human adult VOTC. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication draws the same high-confidence conclusion about large-scale dissociation of face and word selectivity in VOTC, matching the preprint conclusion closely.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: About 70% of significant contacts show either only face-selectivity or only word-selectivity, with the expected right and left hemispheric dominance, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: About 70% of significant electrode contacts show either face-selectivity or word-selectivity only. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric result and basic pattern (≈70% single-category contacts) are preserved; the publication omits the hemisphere dominance detail but the core result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only 30% of the significant contacts show both face- and word-selective responses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the 30% of overlapping face- and word-selective contacts, between-category-selective amplitudes show no-to-weak correlations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication references the same 30% overlap; while it pairs that with correlation results, the numeric overlap result is retained and thus the preprint claim maps closely.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Between-category selective-amplitudes (faces vs. words) showed no-to-weak correlations, despite strong correlations in within-category selective amplitudes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the 30% of overlapping face- and word-selective contacts, between-category-selective amplitudes show no-to-weak correlations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the between-category amplitude correlation result for the overlapping contacts verbatim, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Spatial dissociations are found within core regions of face and word processing, with a medio-lateral dissociation in the fusiform gyrus and surrounding sulci. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a specific spatial-dissociation result (medio-lateral dissociation within core regions) that is not present in the preprint claims, so this is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Displaced communication readily evolved in foraging agents endowed with neural networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Displaced communication readily evolved in foraging agents endowed with neural networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result verbatim, with no change in scope or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Agents did not use signal amplitude to convey information on food location, but instead relied on signal onset-delay and duration-based communication. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Agents did not use signal amplitude to convey information on food location, but instead relied on signal onset-delay and duration-based modes of communication. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same observation with only minor wording differences, keeping scope and confidence the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Communication via signal amplitude, although more efficient, took more generations to evolve compared to communication based on onset-delay and length of signaling. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mode of communication based on signal amplitude was more efficient and led to higher performance. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the efficiency claim about amplitude but drops the generational/time-to-evolve claim and reframes it as a performance result, so the original evolutionary-timing statement is not carried forward.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Displaced communication is likely to initially evolve from non-communicative behavioral cues before leading to more efficient communication systems through a ritualization process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Displaced communication is likely to initially evolve from non-communicative behavioral cues, with evolution leading to more efficient communication systems through a ritualization process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion and reasoning about ritualization and origin from non-communicative cues with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNNs have achieved very high object categorization performance, with some exceeding human performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or endorse claims about CNNs exceeding human performance; that result is absent in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a brain-CNN correspondence in a number of CNNs, with lower and higher levels of visual representations in the human brain resembling those of lower and higher CNN layers, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not present an explicit claim of layerwise correspondence as stated in the preprint; this specific correspondence claim is not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No CNN examined could fully capture the representational structure of higher human visual processing areas. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNNs do not fully capture higher level visual representations of real-world objects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core negative result but splits it by stimulus type (real-world vs. artificial), which narrows/clarifies the original general claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNNs likely represent visual information in fundamentally different ways from the human brain. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Some fundamental differences exist in how the brain and CNNs represent visual information. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses essentially the same conclusion about fundamental representational differences with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fluctuations of cortical excitability demonstrated long-range temporal dependencies decaying according to a power law across trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Fluctuations of cortical excitability demonstrated long-range temporal dependencies decaying according to a power law across trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same empirical finding with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We establish a mechanistic link between ongoing and evoked activity through cortical excitability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We establish a mechanistic link between ongoing and evoked activity through cortical excitability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The mechanistic link claim is carried forward without alteration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Criticality may represent a parsimonious organizing principle of variability in stimulus-related brain processes on a cortical level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Criticality may represent a parsimonious organizing principle of variability in stimulus-related brain processes on a cortical level. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The interpretive suggestion about criticality is preserved verbatim and with the same uncertainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No signatures of criticality were found in subcortical or peripheral nerve activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No signatures of criticality were found in subcortical or peripheral nerve activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The negative result for subcortical/peripheral signals is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Perceptual confidence can be dissociated from the probability of being correct. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Recent behavioral results indicate that confidence and the probability of being correct can be dissociated. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames this as indicated by behavioral results (so clarifies and contextualizes the claim) and reports a lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A leaky competing accumulator neural network model can account for counterintuitive findings where confidence and decision accuracy dissociate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrate that this biologically plausible model can account for several counterintuitive findings reported in the literature where confidence and decision accuracy dissociate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication demonstrates the same modeling result and retains the claim and its certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Systematic variability in normalization tuning exists in both sensory cortices and decision-making circuitry. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A full complement of behavioral data across several previously published experimental results is best accounted for when confidence is computed from units without, rather than units with, tuned inhibition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes a modeling explanation (units without tuned inhibition) that accounts for behavioral patterns; this clarifies and reframes the empirical statement about normalization tuning variability into an explanatory/modeling claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The brain implements a heuristic theory of perceptual confidence computation by utilizing the diversity of neural resources. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings suggest that the brain has developed and implements this alternative, heuristic theory of perceptual confidence computation by relying on the diversity of neural resources available. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reaches the same heuristic-conclusion but with reduced stated certainty and more cautious phrasing, so the claim is clarified and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using an artificial neural network (ANN) analysis improves the accuracy of NIRS models for age-grading malaria transmitting mosquitoes compared to partial least squares (PLS) regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The artificial neural network (ANN) regression model achieved a lower root mean squared error (RMSE) compared to the PLS regression model for both An. gambiae and An. arabiensis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same improvement in regression performance (lower RMSE), supporting the preprint's claim of improved accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A directly trained binary classifier yields higher age estimation accuracy than a regression model interpreted as a binary classifier. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A directly trained binary classifier yields higher age estimation accuracy than a regression model interpreted as a binary classifier. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this specific comparative result verbatim with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Training both regression and binary classification age models using ANNs results in higher estimation accuracies than when trained using PLS. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of the ANN regression model as a binary classifier was significantly higher than that of the PLS regression model when interpreting regression models as binary classifiers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific statistical comparisons (e.g., ANN regression-as-classifier better than PLS) that support and clarify the broader preprint conclusion about ANN training superiority.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A directly trained ANN binary classifier scored an accuracy of 99.4 ± 1.0% for An. gambiae and 99.0 ± 0.6% for An. arabiensis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The directly trained ANN binary classifier scored an accuracy of 99.4% for An. gambiae and 99.0% for An. arabiensis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same high accuracies (omitting the reported uncertainty in the phrasing), so the numeric result is preserved with slight wording change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: It is recommended to train models to estimate age of An. gambiae and An. arabiensis using ANN model architectures and direct training of binary classifiers. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: It is recommended to use ANN model architectures and direct training of binary classifiers for estimating the age of An. arabiensis and An. gambiae. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation is preserved but presented with stronger certainty in the publication, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Behavioral judgments revealed a coarse division between man-made and natural images, with clear groupings of conceptually-related categories.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Behavioral judgments primarily revealed a coarse division between man-made and natural images.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core claim about a coarse man-made vs. natural division but omits the additional detail about clear groupings of conceptually-related categories, so the claim is clarified and somewhat narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: fMRI responses tended to reflect a separation of both human and non-human faces/bodies from all other categories.
alignments.pub_claim_text: fMRI responses primarily reflected a separation of both human and non-human faces/bodies from all other categories.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with equivalent emphasis and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is no simple mapping between responses in high-level visual cortex and behavior, as each domain reflects different visual properties of the images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Responses in high-level visual cortex may correspond to intermediate stages of processing between basic visual features and the conceptual categories that dominate the behavioral response.
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts a lack of simple mapping; the publication frames a specific interpretation (intermediate processing stages) and uses softened language ('may'), altering and narrowing the original conclusion and lowering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both behavioral judgments and fMRI representations showed good correspondence with a deep neural network, but each correlated most strongly with different layers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Comparison of the behavioral and fMRI representational spaces with those derived from the layers of a deep neural network showed a strong correspondence with behavior in the top-most layer and with fMRI in the mid-level layers.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies which DNN layers correspond to behavior and fMRI, making the preprint's more general statement more specific (clarified/narrowed).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings highlight that great care must be taken in mapping the response of visual cortex onto behavior, which reflect different information.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Responses in high-level visual cortex may correspond to intermediate stages of processing between basic visual features and the conceptual categories that dominate the behavioral response.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys a more specific and tentative interpretation for why cortex and behavior differ; this clarifies and narrows the preprint's cautionary conclusion and reduces certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Uncertainpy is an open-source Python toolbox designed for uncertainty quantification and sensitivity analysis of neuroscience models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Uncertainpy is an open-source Python toolbox designed for uncertainty quantification and sensitivity analysis of neuroscience models.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution statement verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Uncertainpy allows for uncertainty quantification and sensitivity analysis to be performed on existing models without modifying the model equations or implementation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Uncertainpy allows for uncertainty quantification and sensitivity analysis to be performed on existing models without modifying the model equations or implementation.
alignments.rationale: Identical claim about workflow usage is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The toolbox utilizes polynomial chaos expansions, which are more efficient than standard Monte-Carlo based approaches.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Uncertainpy utilizes polynomial chaos expansions, which are more efficient than standard Monte-Carlo based approaches for uncertainty analysis.
alignments.rationale: Same methodological claim; publication wording adds minor specificity but does not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Uncertainpy can calculate the uncertainty and sensitivity of salient model response features relevant for various neural and neural network models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The toolbox can calculate the uncertainty and sensitivity of salient model response features relevant for various neural and neural network models.
alignments.rationale: Functionality claim is preserved with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The behavioral signatures of human face perception reflect and are well explained as the result of optimization for the task of face recognition.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The behavioral signatures of human face perception reflect optimization for the task of face recognition.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the central conclusion equivalently (minor wording differences do not change meaning).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNNs trained on face recognition exhibit the same behavioral signatures as human face perception, while CNNs trained on object recognition do not.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep convolutional neural networks trained on face recognition exhibit the same behavioral signatures as humans.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the positive result for face-trained CNNs but omits the negative comparison to object-trained models, narrowing the original comparative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The nature of the computations underlying face recognition may not be as 'special' as previously thought.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The nature of the computations underlying face recognition may not be as special as previously thought.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this interpretive conclusion with similar hedging and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classic signatures of human face perception emerge spontaneously in CNNs trained on face discrimination, but not in those trained on object classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNNs trained on car discrimination also show a drop in performance for inverted vs. upright car images, similar to the face inversion effect.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports additional cases (e.g., car discrimination) that conflict with the preprint's general negative statement about object-trained models, revising the original claim about absence of such signatures in object-trained networks.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work illustrates how CNN models can provide psychological insights into human perception by linking to classic behavioral findings in vision research.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not explicitly include this broader methodological/conceptual contribution; it is not represented among the publication claims provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed PACESS method effectively classifies hundreds of thousands of cells in thick 3D bone marrow samples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PACESS is a computational workflow for bone marrow analysis that combines convolutional neural networks for 2D cell detection and classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the workflow and its components but removes the specific quantitative claim about classifying hundreds of thousands of cells in thick 3D samples, which narrows the original performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The presence of large clusters of leukemic cells affects the distribution of T cells and megakaryocytes differently.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using PACESS, distinct leukemic clusters generate diverse, previously unrecognized neighborhoods within the same bone marrow cavity.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports that leukemic clusters generate diverse neighborhoods (a more general description) but does not specify the particular effects on T cells and megakaryocytes, so the preprint's specific cellular detail is not preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PACESS can reveal previously unrecognized, unique microenvironments within the same bone marrow cavity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using PACESS, distinct leukemic clusters generate diverse, previously unrecognized neighborhoods within the same bone marrow cavity.
alignments.rationale: This result is essentially restated in the publication with the same meaning (previously unrecognized neighborhoods/microenvironments).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current statistical analyses of cellular localization are unsuitable for investigating interactions between more than two cell types.
alignments.rationale: The provided publication claims do not include this limitation about current statistical analyses, so the preprint's explicit methodological limitation is not represented.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Relations between the physico-chemical descriptors and their contributions to the mutational effects are extracted by analyzing the data on 29,832 variants from 8 systematic deep-mutational scan studies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Relations between the physicochemical descriptors and their contributions to the mutational effects are extracted by analyzing the data on 29,832 variants from eight systematic deep mutational scan studies.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same dataset and result with only minor stylistic changes (e.g., numeral formatting).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: It is found that the intuitive dependences of fitness and solubility on the distance of the amino acid from the active site could be extracted and quantified.
alignments.pub_claim_text: An opposite trend in the dependence of fitness and solubility on the distance of the amino acid from the catalytic sites could be extracted and quantified.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a qualitatively different (opposite) trend regarding dependence on distance from catalytic sites, which revises the direction of the original finding while keeping the same scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dependence of the mutational effect contributions on the number of contacts an amino acid has or the BLOSUM score descriptor of the change showed universal trends.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dependence of the mutational effect contributions on the position-specific scoring matrix (PSSM) score for the amino acid after mutation or the BLOSUM score of the substitution showed universal trends.
alignments.rationale: The publication replaces one descriptor (number of contacts) with PSSM as showing universal trends; this alters which features are implicated and thus revises the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our attempts in the present work to explain the quantitative differences in the dependence on conservation and SASA across proteins were not successful.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our attempts in the present work to explain the quantitative differences in the dependence on conservation and SASA across proteins were not successful.
alignments.rationale: The limitation/negative result is stated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The work brings transparency into the predictions, development of rules, and will hopefully lead to uncovering the universalities among these rules.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work brings transparency into the predictions and development of rules, and will hopefully lead to empirically uncovering the universalities among these rules.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the qualifier 'empirically' to the hopeful outcome, a small clarification without substantive scope change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed unsupervised approach using cycleGAN can segment true images effectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach successfully segments bright-field images of cell cultures, images from a live/dead assay of C. elegans, and X-ray computed tomography of metallic nanowire meshes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides concrete examples of image types successfully segmented, which clarifies and narrows the preprint's general claim that the approach segments true images effectively.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The unsupervised method is competitive with a state-of-the-art supervised cell-counting network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed unsupervised approach using computer-generated images for segmentation is competitive with a state-of-the-art supervised cell-counting network. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the competitiveness claim but adds that the method uses computer-generated images for segmentation, a clarifying implementation detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method can precisely locate individual cells in various imaging contexts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method is able to precisely locate individual cells in various types of images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result about precise localization of individual cells with no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Labeling data for training AI algorithms is a limiting factor in traditional methods. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include or reiterate this limitation claim about labeled training data, so the preprint claim is not represented in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The QUEEN model successfully learned to distinguish multimers from monomers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The QUEEN model successfully learned to distinguish multimers from monomers and predicts the specific quaternary state with moderate success. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same primary result that QUEEN distinguishes multimers from monomers without changing scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The specific quaternary state is predicted with moderate success by the QUEEN model, better than a simple model that transfers annotation based on sequence similarity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The QUEEN model successfully learned to distinguish multimers from monomers and predicts the specific quaternary state with moderate success. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the moderate success claim for predicting quaternary state but omits the comparative statement that it outperforms a simple transfer-by-sequence model, effectively tempering that comparative claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: QUEEN is the first to investigate the power of embeddings for the prediction of the quaternary state of proteins. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: QUEEN is the first to investigate the power of embeddings for the prediction of the quaternary state of proteins. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the novelty/contribution claim identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The QUEEN model lays out the strength as well as limitations of a sequence-based protein language model approach compared to structure-based approaches. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: QUEEN performs worse than approaches that include information from solved crystal structures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes a specific limitation (worse performance versus structure-informed methods), which is a stronger, more specific articulation of the limitations noted in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The QUEEN model does not require any structural information and is fast, making it potentially useful for both in-depth investigation of specific systems and studies of large sets of protein sequences. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The QUEEN model does not require any structural information and is fast, making it suitable for wide use in protein sequence studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the non-reliance on structure and speed claims but frames suitability for wide use more concisely; the essence is retained with similar confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Complex, quaternary state related information is included in ESM-2 embeddings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts that ESM-2 embeddings encode complex quaternary-state information, a result not present in the preprint and thus added.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepBryo is capable of detecting objects belonging to six classes and outputting fourteen morphological shape measurements for each object based on the inferred segmentation maps. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepBryo is capable of detecting objects belonging to six classes and outputting 14 morphological shape measurements for each object. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the exact capability and output specifications from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model shows high (>0.8) recall and precision for zooid-level structures with a low misclassification rate of approximately 4%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model shows high (> 0.8) recall and precision for zooid‐level structures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the high recall and precision result; numerical specifics are consistent with the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepBryo reduces the person-hours required for characterizing the zooids in individual colonies to less than 1% of the time required for manual annotation at no significant loss of measurement accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepBryo reduces the person‐hours required for characterizing individual colonies to less than 1% of the time required for manual annotation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same time-saving result as the preprint with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results indicate that DeepBryo enables cost-, labor-, and time-efficient morphometric characterization of cheilostome bryozoans. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepBryo enables cost‐, labor‐, and time‐efficient morphometric characterization of cheilostome bryozoans. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about efficiency without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DeepBryo can greatly increase the scale of macroevolutionary, ecological, taxonomic, and paleobiological analyses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DeepBryo can greatly increase the scale of macroevolutionary, ecological, taxonomic, and paleobiological analyses. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same prospective impact claim as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EyeAge predicted chronological age more accurately than other aging clocks, with a mean absolute error of 2.86 and 3.30 years on quality-filtered data from EyePACS and UK Biobank, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The retinal aging clock, 'eyeAge', predicted chronological age more accurately than other aging clocks with a mean absolute error of 2.86 and 3.30 years on quality-filtered data from EyePACS and UK Biobank, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance metrics and comparative accuracy claim from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EyeAgeAccel was highly independent of blood marker-based measures of biological age, maintaining an all-cause mortality hazard ratio of 1.026 even in the presence of phenotypic age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EyeAge was independent of blood marker-based measures of biological age, maintaining an all-cause mortality hazard ratio of 1.026 even when adjusted for phenotypic age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same independence result and hazard ratio, framed in equivalent terms.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Longitudinal studies showed that the resulting models were able to predict individuals’ aging on time-scales less than a year, with 71% accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific longitudinal prediction result (71% accuracy for <1-year timescales), so this preprint claim is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicted age from retinal images can be used as a biomarker of biological aging that is independent from assessment based on blood markers. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: EyeAge was independent of blood marker-based measures of biological age, maintaining an all-cause mortality hazard ratio of 1.026 even when adjusted for phenotypic age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's independence result supports the preprint's conclusion that retinal-predicted age can serve as a blood-marker-independent biomarker; the substantive claim is retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study demonstrates the potential utility of a retinal aging clock for studying aging and age-related diseases and quantitatively measuring aging on very short time-scales. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the potential utility of a retinal aging clock for studying aging and age-related diseases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the utility claim but omits the explicit phrase about measuring aging on very short time-scales; the general conclusion is preserved though slightly less specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dataset contains projected yields of maize, rice, soybean, and wheat in 91 countries under major emission scenarios for the 21st century. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dataset contains projected yields of four major crops in 91 countries under major emission scenarios for the 21st century. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This dataset provides a basis for a comprehensive understanding of the impacts of climate change on crop production. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This dataset provides a solid basis for a quantitative assessment of the impacts of climate change on crop production. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the claim from a broad 'comprehensive understanding' to a more specific 'quantitative assessment' and expresses higher certainty, indicating clarification and a narrowed scope with amplified confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dataset will facilitate the rapidly developing data-driven machine learning applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The dataset will facilitate the rapidly developing data-driven machine learning applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim about facilitating machine learning applications is identical between preprint and publication with no substantive change in wording or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Polarimetric second-harmonic generation (P-SHG) microscopy enables label-free visualization and ultrastructural investigation of non-centrosymmetric molecules. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Polarimetric second-harmonic generation (P-SHG) microscopy enables label-free visualization and ultrastructural investigation of non-centrosymmetric molecules. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The P-SHG parameters can differentiate tumor tissue from normal tissue with 94.2% accuracy and F1-score, and a 6.3% false discovery rate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The P-SHG parameters can differentiate tumor from normal tissue with 94.2% accuracy and F1-score, and a 6.3% false discovery rate. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained classifier predicts tumor tissue with 91.3% accuracy, 90.7% F1-score, and a 13.8% false omission rate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained classifier predicts tumor tissue with 91.3% accuracy, 90.7% F1-score, and a 13.8% false omission rate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance metrics and claim are identical between preprint and published versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Widefield P-SHG microscopy reveals collagen ultrastructure over large tissue regions and can be utilized as a sensitive biomarker for cancer diagnostics and prognostics studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Widefield P-SHG microscopy reveals collagen ultrastructure over large tissue regions and can be utilized as a sensitive biomarker for cancer diagnostics and prognostics studies. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Genome editing can induce various unintended events such as structural variations, small indels, and substitutions at, and in some cases, away from the target site. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Current genotyping methods do not allow a comprehensive analysis of diverse mutations for phasing and mosaic variant detection. (certainty=high, type=limitation)

(certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We developed a genotyping method named Determine Allele mutations and Judge Intended genotype by Nanopore sequencer (DAJIN) that can automatically identify and classify both intended and unintended diverse mutations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DAJIN can handle approximately 100 samples under different editing conditions in a single run. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DAJIN can handle approximately 100 samples under different editing conditions in a single run. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DAJIN-assisted multiplex genotyping may become a new standard for validating genome editing outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DAJIN-assisted multiplex genotyping may become a new standard for validating genome editing outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking conclusion about DAJIN's potential role is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The current study modeled the geographic suitability of anthrax across India using a biogeographical application of machine learning. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Water-soil balance, soil chemistry, and historical forest loss were also influential in determining anthrax risk. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both biotic and abiotic features contributed to risk across multiple scales of influence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest that the wildlife-livestock interface plays an important role in the cycling of anthrax in India. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings suggest that the elephant-livestock interface plays an important role in the cycling of anthrax in India. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prevention efforts targeted toward the wildlife-livestock interface may yield successes in reducing ongoing transmission between animal hosts and subsequent zoonotic transmission to humans. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Livestock prevention efforts targeting the elephant-livestock interface may yield successes in reducing ongoing transmission between animal hosts and subsequent zoonotic transmission to humans. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Images of low quality are a source of variability that may be comparable to the effect size under study. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The MRIQC Web-API is an open crowdsourced database that collects image quality metrics extracted from MR images and corresponding manual assessments by experts. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The database currently contains over 100,000 records of image quality metrics of functional and anatomical MRIs of the human brain, and over 200 expert ratings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The database currently contains over 100,000 records of image quality metrics of functional and anatomical MRIs of the human brain, and over 200 expert ratings. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The resource is designed for researchers to share image quality metrics and annotations that can readily be reused in training human experts and machine learning algorithms. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The ultimate goal of the database is to allow the development of fully automated quality control tools that outperform expert ratings in identifying subpar images. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Synonymous mutations in three high-scoring nucleotide sites enhanced the pathogenicity of the influenza A(H1N1) viruses in mice. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The resistance profile of infections can be predicted based on patient-specific demographics and clinical history. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The method allows for the direct study of the capacity of full TCR repertoires to target specific epitopes of relevant vaccines or pathogens. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models can predict 16 closely-related growth conditions using E. coli transcript and protein abundances. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The DeepVariant tool has great potential and usefulness for analysis of WGS data in medical genetics. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
